facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The speaker asks if the listener knows who they are — か embeds the identity question inside the larger sentence.

facts.quizQuestion:
今度の出張はいつ{___}まだ未定です。
(Regarding my next business trip, [when] it will be is still undecided.)
facts.explanation: か (whether, the question of (sub sentence question)) — 今度の出張はいつかまだ分かりません。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The timing of the trip is still unknown. か turns 'when' into an embedded question that the speaker can't yet answer.

facts.quizQuestion:
誰が一緒に出掛けた{___}教えてください。
(Please tell me [who] you went out with.)
facts.explanation: か (whether, the question of (sub sentence question)) — 誰が一緒に出掛けたか教えてください。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Asking who went out with them — か turns the who-question into a clause that the listener is asked to explain.

facts.quizQuestion:
書類どこにある{___}(と)聞きました。
(I asked, [where] the documents are.)
facts.explanation: か (whether, the question of (sub sentence question)) — 書類どこにあるか(と)聞きました。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The speaker asked where the documents are — か embeds that where-question as reported speech inside the verb 聞いた.

facts.quizQuestion:
バカ{___}ことはやめろ！
(Cut out the [stupid] stuff!)
facts.explanation: な (Vru, (negative command - sentence ending particle)) — バカなことするな！
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: A blunt stop command — attached to the verb stem, it tells someone to simply not do the stupid thing they're about to do.

facts.quizQuestion:
あまり飲む{___}
([Don't] drink too much.)
facts.explanation: な (Vru, (negative command - sentence ending particle)) — あまり飲むな
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: A casual spoken warning to stop before going too far — attached to the verb to cut the action short.

facts.quizQuestion:
馬鹿{___}ことはよせ。
(Knock off the [stupid] behavior.)
facts.explanation: な (Vru, (negative command - sentence ending particle)) — 馬鹿なことをするな。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: A direct order to quit fooling around — the speaker is telling someone to stop acting like an idiot, right now.

facts.quizQuestion:
誰も動く{___}。
(Nobody [moves]!)
facts.explanation: な (Vru, (negative command - sentence ending particle)) — 誰も動くな。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: ということですね (と云う事ですね)
facts.grammarNote: An authoritative freeze command — the speaker is ordering everyone present to stop moving immediately.

facts.quizQuestion:
心配する{___}。
([Don't] worry)
facts.explanation: な (Vru, (negative command - sentence ending particle)) — 心配するな。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: A gentle but direct spoken command — the speaker is telling someone to stop worrying, plain and simple.

facts.quizQuestion:
３月１日の{___}都合がよいのですが。
(We feel that March 1 would be [more] convenient.)
facts.explanation: 方が ((often it'd be better to do __) Such-and-such an option is something) — ３月１日の方が都合がよいのですが。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Points to one option as the more convenient side of a comparison — the speaker is expressing a preference for a specific date.

facts.quizQuestion:
９時以降に電話した{___}安いですか。
(Is it [cheaper] to call after 9:00?)
facts.explanation: 方が ((often it'd be better to do __) Such-and-such an option is something) — ９時以降に電話した方が安いですか。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Sets up a comparison between two options — calling after 9 is the side being tested as the cheaper one.

facts.quizQuestion:
物事を生半可に知っているくらいなら全然知らない{___}ましだ。
(I would [rather] not know a thing at all than know it imperfectly.)
facts.explanation: 方が ((often it'd be better to do __) Such-and-such an option is something) — 物事を生半可に知っているくらいなら全然知らない方がましだ。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: と伝えていただけませんか
facts.grammarNote: Weighs two states against each other — knowing nothing is judged to be the better side compared to knowing something poorly.

facts.quizQuestion:
遅くても着実な{___}競争に勝つ。
(Slow [but sure] wins the race.)
facts.explanation: 方が ((often it'd be better to do __) Such-and-such an option is something) — 遅くても着実な方が競争に勝つ。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Picks one side of a contrast as the winner — slow but steady is positioned as the preferable approach over speed alone.

facts.quizQuestion:
私は肉より魚の{___}いい。
(I [prefer] fish to meat.)
facts.explanation: 方が ((often it'd be better to do __) Such-and-such an option is something) — 私は肉より魚の方がいい。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Marks what you're choosing over the other option — fish is the preferred side when compared to meat.

facts.quizQuestion:
私は肉{___}魚の方がいい。
(I prefer fish [to] meat.)
facts.explanation: より (than, rather than) — 私は肉より魚の方がいい。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Sets meat as the thing being passed over — fish wins when these two options are weighed against each other.

facts.quizQuestion:
現実は聞いた{___}ももっと悲しい。
(The reality is much sadder [than] what I have heard.)
facts.explanation: より (than, rather than) — 現実は聞いたよりももっと悲しい。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Uses what was heard as the lower bar — reality is judged to fall on the more extreme side of that comparison.

facts.quizQuestion:
これ{___}ほかの方法がないんだよ
(There's really no other better way [than] this.)
facts.explanation: より (than, rather than) — これよりほかの方法がないんだよ
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Places all other methods at a lower rank — this one is positioned as the best available option.

facts.quizQuestion:
一般に日本人は、自分たちがかつて{___}暮し向きがよくなったと考えている。
(The Japanese at large consider themselves better off [than] they used to be.)
facts.explanation: より (than, rather than) — 一般に日本人は、自分たちがかつてより暮し向きがよくなったと考えている。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Uses the past as the comparison point — how well-off people are now is measured against how they used to live.

facts.quizQuestion:
彼はわたし{___}速く走ることができる。
(He is able to run faster [than] I am.)
facts.explanation: より (than, rather than) — 彼はわたしより速く走ることができる。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Takes the speaker's speed as the reference point — he is on the faster side when the two are compared.

facts.quizQuestion:
動かない{___}
([Seems like] it doesn't work (lit: doesn't move)
facts.explanation: らしい (seem, look like, apparently, I heard) — 動かないらしい
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: States an apparent conclusion from evidence — the speaker observes that it doesn't seem to be working.

facts.quizQuestion:
そんな事をするなんて君{___}くない。
(It doesn't [seem like] you doing such a thing.)
facts.explanation: らしい (seem, look like, apparently, I heard) — そんな事をするなんて君らしくない。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: と伝えていただけませんか
facts.grammarNote: Used to say something doesn't fit someone's character — doing such a thing seems out of place for who you are.

facts.quizQuestion:
私は彼と彼のガールフレンド{___}人を見かけた。
(I saw him and a girl who [looked to be] his girlfriend.)
facts.explanation: らしい (seem, look like, apparently, I heard) — 私は彼と彼のガールフレンドらしい人を見かけた。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Draws a conclusion from visual observation — based on her appearance and being with him, she seemed to be his girlfriend.

facts.quizQuestion:
みんなさんは夕方来る{___}。
(It [seems] everybody will come in the evening.)
facts.explanation: らしい (seem, look like, apparently, I heard) — みんなさんは夕方来るらしい。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: ということですね (と云う事ですね)
facts.grammarNote: Reports an expectation based on available information — it seems likely, based on what you know, that everyone is coming.

facts.quizQuestion:
彼は分かり{___}でしょうかなぁ？
(He's [difficult to understand], isn't he?)
facts.explanation: にくい (Is difficult to do) — 彼は分かりにくいでしょうかなぁ？
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Expresses that understanding this person is genuinely difficult — it's a built-in challenge, not just occasional confusion.

facts.quizQuestion:
食べ{___}食べ物がきらいだ。
(I don't like [difficult to eat] foods.)
facts.explanation: にくい (Is difficult to do) — 食べにくい食べ物がきらいだ。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Marks food that poses a practical difficulty to eat — the difficulty is a property of the food itself.

facts.quizQuestion:
このめずらしい切手は手にはいり{___}んだよ。
(This rare stamp is [hard to come by].)
facts.explanation: にくい (Is difficult to do) — このめずらしい切手は手にはいりにくいんだよ。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Describes the stamp as something hard to obtain — acquiring it is the inherently difficult action.

facts.quizQuestion:
タイガーティの説明はとてもわかり{___}ですね｡
(tigert`s explanation is very [difficult to understand].)
facts.explanation: にくい (Is difficult to do) — タイガーティの説明はとてもわかりにくいですね｡
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Evaluates the explanation as objectively hard to follow — the difficulty is a quality of the explanation, not the listener.

facts.quizQuestion:
英語での本を読み{___}です。
(It's [hard to read] the book in English.)
facts.explanation: にくい (Is difficult to do) — 英語での本を読みにくいです。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: States that reading in English presents a real difficulty — the challenge comes from the language barrier itself.

facts.quizQuestion:
彼は日本に住んでいたくせに、日本語を話せる{___}らなかったよ。
(Although he lived in Japan he didn't learn [become able] to speak Japanese!)
facts.explanation: ようになる (Indicates a change which has taken place or will take place.) — 彼は日本に住んでいたくせに、日本語を話せるようにならなかったよ。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: He lived in Japan but never made the change to speaking Japanese. ようになる marks this as a shift in ability that was expected but didn't happen.

facts.quizQuestion:
子供は, 自分で部屋を片付ける{___}りました。
((My) child has [reached the point where] she cleans up her room herself.)
facts.explanation: ようになる (Indicates a change which has taken place or will take place.) — 子供は, 自分で部屋を片付けるようになりました。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: と伝えていただけませんか
facts.grammarNote: The child reached a new stage — now she does it herself. ようになる marks this as a gradual change into a new habit.

facts.quizQuestion:
赤ちゃんは, もうすぐ笑う{___}。
(The baby will soon [reach the point where] he will smile.)
facts.explanation: ようになる (Indicates a change which has taken place or will take place.) — 赤ちゃんは, もうすぐ笑うようになる。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The baby hasn't started smiling yet, but soon will reach that point. ようになる describes the coming shift into a new behavior.

facts.quizQuestion:
日本に来たから、豆腐を食べる{___}りました。
(Since I came to Japan, I've [gotten used to] eating tofu. (I didn't use to eat tofu.)
facts.explanation: ようになる (Indicates a change which has taken place or will take place.) — 日本に来たから、豆腐を食べるようになりました。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Coming to Japan brought a change — eating tofu became normal now. ようになる captures that transition from 'didn't do it' to 'now does it.'

facts.quizQuestion:
レンズさんは日本語が話せる{___}った。
(Mr. Renz had [reached the point where] he can speak Japanese.)
facts.explanation: ようになる (Indicates a change which has taken place or will take place.) — レンズさんは日本語が話せるようになった。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Mr. Renz reached the point of being able to speak Japanese. ようになる marks that he got there through gradual change.

facts.quizQuestion:
速さという点では飛行機は船とは{___}。
(When we talk about speed, [there is no point to compare] a plane and a ship.)
facts.explanation: 比べものにならない (there is no point comparing....) — 速さという点では飛行機は船とは比べものにならない。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.reading: くらべものにならない
facts.pronunciation: くらべものにならない
facts.grammarNote: Treats speed as a dimension where the gap between a plane and a ship is simply too large to make the comparison meaningful.

facts.quizQuestion:
食べる量という点では馬は鳥とは{___}。
(What a horse eats and what a chicken eats just [cannot be compared].)
facts.explanation: 比べものにならない (there is no point comparing....) — 食べる量という点では馬は鳥とは比べものにならない。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.reading: くらべものにならない
facts.pronunciation: くらべものにならない
facts.grammarNote: Points out that what a horse eats versus what a bird eats are so different in scale that comparing them is pointless.

facts.quizQuestion:
このほうが{___}ほど優れている。
(This is superior [beyond comparison].)
facts.explanation: 比べものにならない (there is no point comparing....) — このほうが比べものにならないほど優れている。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.reading: くらべものにならない
facts.pronunciation: くらべものにならない
facts.grammarNote: States that the superiority is so absolute that there's nothing worth comparing — it's beyond any contest.

facts.quizQuestion:
私の業績はあなたの業績とは{___}。
(Your achievements [cannot be compared] with mine.)
facts.explanation: 比べものにならない (there is no point comparing....) — 私の業績はあなたの業績とは比べものにならない。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.reading: くらべものにならない
facts.pronunciation: くらべものにならない
facts.grammarNote: Dismisses the idea of comparing the two sets of achievements — they are on such different levels that any comparison is meaningless.

facts.quizQuestion:
私の料理などお母さんのとは{___}。
(My cooking [cannot hold a candle to] Mother's.)
facts.explanation: 比べものにならない (there is no point comparing....) — 私の料理などお母さんのとは比べものにならない。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.reading: くらべものにならない
facts.pronunciation: くらべものにならない
facts.grammarNote: Concedes that the speaker's own cooking is so far below their mother's that even trying to compare would be unfair.

facts.quizQuestion:
今晩は暖かいので、ストーブが要らない{___}だ。
(It's [so] warm this evening [that] we almost don't need a heater.)
facts.explanation: くらい、ぐらい (位) (to the extent that; so ~ that ~ almost ~; at least; the only ~; rather than ~) — 今晩は暖かいので、ストーブが要らないくらいだ。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: It's warm to the degree that a heater is almost unnecessary — くらい measures how warm the evening is by expressing what that warmth equals.

facts.quizQuestion:
私たちは一歩も歩けない{___}疲れていた。
(We were [so] tired [that] we couldn't take a single step.)
facts.explanation: くらい、ぐらい (位) (to the extent that; so ~ that ~ almost ~; at least; the only ~; rather than ~) — 私たちは一歩も歩けないくらい疲れていた。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The tiredness was so extreme that walking even one step was impossible — くらい conveys the degree of exhaustion through its effect.

facts.quizQuestion:
山田さん{___}物を忘れる人はいない。
(There's no one [as] forgetful as Yamada (who forgets things as much as Yamada).)
facts.explanation: くらい、ぐらい (位) (to the extent that; so ~ that ~ almost ~; at least; the only ~; rather than ~) — 山田さんくらい物を忘れる人はいない。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Yamada is the benchmark for forgetfulness — no one forgets things to the degree Yamada does. くらい sets the scale.

facts.quizQuestion:
私は料理は下手ですが、ご飯{___}(は)炊けます。
(I'm a poor cook, but I can [at least] cook rice.)
facts.explanation: くらい、ぐらい (位) (to the extent that; so ~ that ~ almost ~; at least; the only ~; rather than ~) — 私は料理は下手ですが、ご飯くらい(は)炊けます。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Cooking rice is the modest lower limit of what the speaker can do — くらい here marks the minimum level of ability.

facts.quizQuestion:
今この仕事が出来るのは彼{___}のものだ。
(He's the only person who can do this job now. (The person who's capable [enough] to do this job is he.)
facts.explanation: くらい、ぐらい (位) (to the extent that; so ~ that ~ almost ~; at least; the only ~; rather than ~) — 今この仕事が出来るのは彼くらいのものだ。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: He is the only person capable enough for this job right now — くらい here marks his skill level as the threshold that fits.

facts.quizQuestion:
日本語を覚える{___}、時間がかかります
(It takes time [in order to] learn japanese)
facts.explanation: のに (in order to, to) — 日本語を覚えるのに、時間がかかります
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Learning Japanese requires time — のに marks learning Japanese as the purpose or goal for which time is needed.

facts.quizQuestion:
あなたに会う{___}は何時(いつ）/何処(どこ）がいいですか。
(What would be the good time/place [in order to] see you?)
facts.explanation: のに (in order to, to) — あなたに会うのには何時(いつ）/何処(どこ）がいいですか。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: A good time and place are needed in order to meet you — のに marks meeting as the goal the time and place are required for.

facts.quizQuestion:
パスポートは海外旅行に行く{___}必要です。
(A passport is necessary [in order to] travel overseas.)
facts.explanation: のに (in order to, to) — パスポートは海外旅行に行くのに必要です。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: A passport is what you need in order to travel overseas — のに marks traveling as the purpose the passport is necessary for.

facts.quizQuestion:
水着は泳ぐ{___}使う。
(Swimwear is used [for] swimming.)
facts.explanation: のに (in order to, to) — 水着は泳ぐのに使う。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Swimwear is used for the purpose of swimming — のに marks swimming as the function the item serves.

facts.quizQuestion:
私がその仕事を仕上げる{___}５ヶ月かかった。
(It took me five months [to] complete the job.)
facts.explanation: のに (in order to, to) — 私がその仕事を仕上げるのに５ヶ月かかった。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: ところに、ところへ、ところを
facts.grammarNote: Five months were needed in order to complete the job — のに marks completing the job as the purpose that required all that time.

facts.quizQuestion:
私は息子がクッキーを盗み食いしようとしている{___}を見つけた。
(I caught my son [stealing] from a cookie jar.)
facts.explanation: ところ(だ) (所だ・処だ) (I am about to / I was about to) — 私は息子がクッキーを盗み食いしようとしているところを見つけた。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: ところに、ところへ、ところを
facts.grammarNote: Catching the son in the act of sneaking cookies happens right at that exact moment — ところ pinpoints the precise instant the action was caught in progress.

facts.quizQuestion:
バスは発車する{___}だった。
(The bus was [about to] start.)
facts.explanation: ところ(だ) (所だ・処だ) (I am about to / I was about to) — バスは発車するところだった。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The bus was on the verge of leaving — ところだった pins the moment to just before departure, capturing the 'about to' timing.

facts.quizQuestion:
これから、部屋を掃除する{___}です。
(I'm just [about to] start cleaning up my room.)
facts.explanation: ところ(だ) (所だ・処だ) (I am about to / I was about to) — これから、部屋を掃除するところです。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: I'm just about to begin cleaning right now — ところです marks the action as imminent, happening at precisely this moment in time.

facts.quizQuestion:
私{___}肉を食べない。
(I don't eat meat. (but I eat fish))
facts.explanation: は ('ha (wa)' used to indicate contrast) — 私は肉は食べない。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: I don't eat meat (but I eat fish) — は creates a contrast, singling out meat as the item being negated while implying other foods are fine.

facts.quizQuestion:
「セリーナをみせて！」「心配{___}要らない。生きているから。」
("Show me Serena!" "No need to worry. She's alive.")
facts.explanation: は ('ha (wa)' used to indicate contrast) — 「セリーナをみせて！」「心配は要らない。いきてはいる、いきて。」
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: She's alive (but not well) — は creates the contrast between being alive and the unstated implication that all is not okay.

facts.quizQuestion:
いや、でも野球を見に行くの{___}好きだけどね。
(No, [but] I like + going to baseball.)
facts.explanation: は ('ha (wa)' used to indicate contrast) — いや、でも野球を見に行くのは好きだけどね。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Liking to go to baseball is set in contrast to what was just said — は marks it as the contrasting element being introduced.

facts.quizQuestion:
私{___}、足し算が好きだけど、引き算が嫌いだ。
(As for me, I like addition but [hate] subtraction.)
facts.explanation: は ('ha (wa)' used to indicate contrast) — 私は、足し算は好きだけど、引き算は嫌いだ。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: I like addition but dislike subtraction — は marks the speaker as the topic, and the two preferences are set in contrast.

facts.quizQuestion:
彼{___}上海で住んでいたので、戦争と無関係なところで暮らしていた。
(Because he was living in Shanghai, he lived in a place [unrelated to] the war.)
facts.explanation: は ('ha (wa)' used to indicate contrast) — 彼は上海で住んでいたので、戦争は彼とは全く関係のないところで行われていた．
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Living in Shanghai placed him apart from the war — は marks him as the topic, contrasting his location with where the war was happening.

facts.quizQuestion:
天気予報を聞いてたら雪が降る{___}です。
(When I listened to the weather forcast they [reckoned] it's going to snow.)
facts.explanation: そう (I hear that) — 天気予報を聞いてたら雪が降るそうです。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: ということですね (と云う事ですね)
facts.grammarNote: Reports what you heard from an external source — the weather forecast said snow, so you're passing that along.

facts.quizQuestion:
電話したら彼はいない{___}です。
(Upon phoning, I was [told] that he wasn't there.)
facts.explanation: そう (I hear that) — 電話したら彼はいないそうです。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Relays information received indirectly — when you called, someone told you he wasn't there.

facts.quizQuestion:
彼はいないのですかと聞いたら{___}だといわれました。
(When I asked if he was not there, I was [told] that it was so.)
facts.explanation: そう (I hear that) — 彼はいないのですかと聞いたらそうだといわれました。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Confirms information that came from another person — you asked, and someone told you that was indeed the case.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___}ですか！
([Is that so]!)
facts.explanation: そう (I hear that) — そうですか！
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: A response confirming received information — 'Is that so?' acknowledges something you just heard.

facts.quizQuestion:
なんで{___}やねん(Kansai Dialect)
(Why [so]?)
facts.explanation: そう (I hear that) — なんでそうやねん(Kansai Dialect)
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Used in Kansai dialect to question a statement — the speaker is pushing back on something they were just told.

facts.quizQuestion:
バスが来る{___}。
(The bus is coming[!])
facts.explanation: よ (! (exclamation mark, add emphasis, denote new information being imparted)) — バスが来るよ。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The speaker is alerting the listener to something happening right now — よ signals new, urgent information being shared.

facts.quizQuestion:
それは大変なことだ{___}。
(That's pretty serious, [you realize that]!)
facts.explanation: よ (! (exclamation mark, add emphasis, denote new information being imparted)) — それは大変なことだよ。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The speaker is asserting the seriousness of the situation directly to the listener — よ pushes the information as something they should register.

facts.quizQuestion:
あなたが好きなんだ{___}
(I like you, [you know]!)
facts.explanation: よ (! (exclamation mark, add emphasis, denote new information being imparted)) — あなたが好きなんだよ
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: A personal confession delivered directly — よ here adds the assertive edge of saying 'I'm telling you this clearly'.

facts.quizQuestion:
好い加減にしなさい{___}。
(Shape up[!])
facts.explanation: よ (! (exclamation mark, add emphasis, denote new information being imparted)) — 好い加減にしなさいよ。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: A direct command with assertive force — よ at the end makes it clear this is being said with intention, not gently.

facts.explanation: よ (! (exclamation mark, add emphasis, denote new information being imparted)) — いいよ。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: A casual but firm agreement — よ gives the response a confident, assertive quality rather than hesitant.

facts.quizQuestion:
医者に控える{___}われているのでお酒が飲めなくて、ウズウズしている。
(My doctor [told me to] lay off the liquor so I have this uneasy impatient feeling.)
facts.explanation: ように言う (V, To say to (do something)) — 医者に控えるように言われているのでお酒が飲めなくて、ウズウズしている。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Reports an instruction given by the doctor — the doctor told the speaker to cut back on drinking.

facts.quizQuestion:
医者は彼に酒を控える{___}忠告した。
(The doctor [advised him to] keep away from drinking.)
facts.explanation: ように言う (V, To say to (do something)) — 医者は彼に酒を控えるように忠告した。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Reports medical advice given to someone else — ように here conveys the content of what the doctor advised him to do.

facts.quizQuestion:
医者なら誰でも君に禁煙する{___}だろう。
(Any doctor will [tell you to] quit smoking.)
facts.explanation: ように言う (V, To say to (do something)) — 医者なら誰でも君に禁煙するように言うだろう。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: States what any doctor would tell you — the content of that instruction is to quit smoking.

facts.quizQuestion:
私は図書館の職員に金曜日締め切りのレポートのためにその本が必要だと言った。そうしたら彼女は借りている人に返却を求める{___}と言った。
(I told the librarian that I needed the book for a report due on Friday; so she [said she would] call it in.)
facts.explanation: ように言う (V, To say to (do something)) — 私は図書館の職員に金曜日締め切りのレポートのためにその本が必要だと言った。そうしたら彼女は借りている人に返却を求めるように言うと言った。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Reports what the librarian said she would do — she said she would tell the borrower to return the book.

facts.quizQuestion:
先生は私たちに毎日教室を掃除する{___}。
(The teacher [tells us to] clean our classroom every day.)
facts.explanation: ように言う (V, To say to (do something)) — 先生は私たちに毎日教室を掃除するように言う。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The teacher gives a standing instruction — every day, the students are told to clean the classroom.

facts.quizQuestion:
雨が降る{___}な音がしました。
(There was a sound [like] rain falling.)
facts.explanation: よう (appearance, looks, seems) — 雨が降るような音がしました。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Describes an auditory impression — the sound gave the speaker the sense it might be rain, though they're not certain.

facts.quizQuestion:
そして、ウッドは「負けたら承知しないぞ」と言う{___}に全員をにらみつけた。
(Then Wood stared at all the team members [like] he was saying "I won't accept it if you're defeated!")
facts.explanation: よう (appearance, looks, seems) — そして、ウッドは「負けたら承知しないぞ」と言うように全員をにらみつけた。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Captures the manner of someone's expression — Wood's glare conveyed a clear, unspoken message to everyone.

facts.quizQuestion:
星の{___}に輝いてる瞳。
(Eyes that sparkle [like] stars.)
facts.explanation: よう (appearance, looks, seems) — 星のように輝いてる瞳。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: しまう (仕舞う・終う・了う)
ということですね (と云う事ですね)
facts.grammarNote: Draws a poetic comparison in appearance — the eyes shine in a way that resembles how stars look.

facts.quizQuestion:
その{___}なこともあるのでしょうね。
(I guess something [like] that could happen.)
facts.explanation: よう (appearance, looks, seems) — そのようなこともあるのでしょうね。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Gently expresses possibility — the speaker imagines that something like that situation could come to pass.

facts.quizQuestion:
安い{___}だ。
([Looks] cheap.)
facts.explanation: よう (appearance, looks, seems) — 安いようだ。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Describes an observable impression — based on its appearance, it looks like it might be cheap.

facts.quizQuestion:
雨が降ってきたなあ{___}飯にしないか
(Hey, it's starting to rain [By the way] you want to go get something to eat?)
facts.explanation: ところで (所で・処で) (by the way) — 雨が降ってきたなあところで飯にしないか
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Mid-conversation the speaker shifts topic to suggest food — ところで signals that clean break to a new subject.

facts.quizQuestion:
私が説得し{___}あいつは耳をかさないだろう。
([No matter how much] I try to persuade him, that guy won't listen.)
facts.explanation: ところで (所で・処で) (by the way) — ( 「ところで」の他の意味 )私が説得したところであいつは耳をかさないだろう.
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: あまり　+　動詞(否定形)
facts.grammarNote: Even trying to persuade him wouldn't work — this alternate usage means 'no matter what' rather than a topic shift.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___}、あなたの仕事は何ですか。
([By the way], what do you do?)
facts.explanation: ところで (所で・処で) (by the way) — ところで、あなたの仕事は何ですか。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Moving from the current conversation to a new question about the person's job — ところで marks the casual topic change.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___}、あなたは宿題をしましたか。
([By the way], have you done your homework?)
facts.explanation: ところで (所で・処で) (by the way) — ところで、あなたは宿題をしましたか。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Pivoting from whatever was being discussed to ask about homework — ところで introduces the new topic politely.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___}、ちょっと君に話がある。
([By the way], I have something to tell you.)
facts.explanation: ところで (所で・処で) (by the way) — ところで、ちょっと君に話がある。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The speaker has something to say and uses ところで to open the new subject naturally.

facts.quizQuestion:
結婚する{___}は大変だ。
([Getting married] is such an effort!)
facts.explanation: の (Turn a phrase ending in a verb into a 'noun phrase') — 結婚するのは大変だ。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Turns the verb phrase 'getting married' into a noun so it can be the subject of 'is an effort.'

facts.quizQuestion:
「さあ、皆さん、正々堂々戦いましょう」全選手が周りに集まる{___}を待って先生が言った。
("Well, everyone, let's play fair and square." the teacher said after waiting for all the players to assemble[+].)
facts.explanation: の (Turn a phrase ending in a verb into a 'noun phrase') — 「さあ、皆さん、正々堂々戦いましょう」全選手が周りに集まるのを待って先生が言った。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Converts the action 'all players assembling' into a noun phrase — the teacher waited for that event to happen.

facts.quizQuestion:
夢に耽ったり、生きることを忘れてしまう{___}はよくない。
([Becoming absorbed in a dream, forgetting to live] is no good.)
facts.explanation: の (Turn a phrase ending in a verb into a 'noun phrase') — 夢に耽ったり、生きることを忘れてしまうのはよくない。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Nominalizes 'forgetting to live' so it can be the subject of the sentence — you're judging an action, not a thing.

facts.quizQuestion:
私も買い物に行く{___}が好きです。
(I also like [to go shopping].)
facts.explanation: の (Turn a phrase ending in a verb into a 'noun phrase') — 私も買い物に行くのが好きです。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Turns 'going shopping' into a noun so it can be what you 'like' — you need a noun after 好きです.

facts.quizQuestion:
アルバイトをしながら学校に行く{___}は大変ですね。
([Having a part time job while going to school] is hard, right?)
facts.explanation: の (Turn a phrase ending in a verb into a 'noun phrase') — アルバイトをしながら学校に行くのは大変ですね。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Makes 'working part-time while going to school' into a noun phrase — that whole situation is what's described as hard.

facts.quizQuestion:
明日は釣れるといい{___}！
(Won't it be great if (we) can fish tommorow[!])
facts.explanation: なあ、な〜、な (Adds emphasis - sentence ending particle; Emphasizes personal desires) — 明日は釣れるといいなあ！
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: と伝えていただけませんか
facts.grammarNote: Wishing out loud that tomorrow's fishing will go well — なあ adds the warm, daydreaming tone of hoping for something good.

facts.quizQuestion:
冷たいビールを飲みたい{___}。
(I'd [really] like a cold beer. / I could [really] go for a cold beer.)
facts.explanation: なあ、な〜、な (Adds emphasis - sentence ending particle; Emphasizes personal desires) — 冷たいビールを飲みたいなあ。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: A personal longing for a cold beer — なあ gives this the sighing, wistful feel of really wanting something in the moment.

facts.quizQuestion:
腹減った{___}。
(I'm [so] hungry.)
facts.explanation: なあ、な〜、な (Adds emphasis - sentence ending particle; Emphasizes personal desires) — 腹減ったなあ。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Voicing hunger in a casual, expressive way — なあ softens it into a musing remark rather than a demand.

facts.quizQuestion:
「一人で間違えずに歌える自信のある人だけ座り{___}。」と指示しました
([The teacher] directed that (just) people who had the confidence that they could sing, on their own, without error should sit down.)
facts.explanation: なさい (Informal Imperative (request form)) — 「一人で間違えずに歌える自信のある人だけ座りなさい。」と指示しました
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: A teacher's direct instruction — attached to the verb to tell students with confidence to perform a specific action.

facts.quizQuestion:
早くし{___}。
([Do] it quickly.)
facts.explanation: なさい (Informal Imperative (request form)) — 早くしなさい。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: ところに、ところへ、ところを
facts.grammarNote: An impatient command to speed things up — the speaker is ordering someone to act quickly, without softening it.

facts.quizQuestion:
好い加減にし{___}よ。
(Shape [up]!)
facts.explanation: なさい (Informal Imperative (request form)) — 好い加減にしなさいよ。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: A frustrated command to get it together — the speaker has had enough and is telling someone to start behaving properly.

facts.quizQuestion:
野菜を食べ{___}！
([Eat] your vegetables!)
facts.explanation: なさい (Informal Imperative (request form)) — 野菜を食べなさい！
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: A firm parent-style command — telling a child to eat something they probably don't want to, right now.

facts.quizQuestion:
座り{___}。
([Sit down].)
facts.explanation: なさい (Informal Imperative (request form)) — 座りなさい。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: A short, direct order to take a seat — no softening, just a simple instruction to sit down.

facts.quizQuestion:
この靴は高い{___}
(I [wonder] if these shoes are expensive)
facts.explanation: かな (I wonder.. (Based on か question particle.)) — この靴は高いかな
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The speaker is looking at the shoes and wondering about the price — not asking someone else, just thinking aloud with uncertainty.

facts.quizQuestion:
締め切りに間に合わなかったらどうなる{___}。
(What if I miss the deadline [I wonder]?)
facts.explanation: かな (I wonder.. (Based on か question particle.)) — 締め切りに間に合わなかったらどうなるかな。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The speaker is worrying about a hypothetical — what would happen if they miss the deadline. かな voices that open-ended personal concern.

facts.quizQuestion:
「彼の病気は重いの{___}。」「そうでないといいね。」
("Is he seriously ill?" "[I hope not]...")
facts.explanation: かな (I wonder.. (Based on か question particle.)) — 「彼の病気は重いのかな。」「そうでないといいね。」
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The speaker wonders about the seriousness of the illness, expressing uncertainty and mild worry to themselves.

facts.quizQuestion:
「肘が痛むんだよ。病院に行こう{___}。」
("My elbow really hurts. [I guess] I should go to a hospital.")
facts.explanation: かな (I wonder.. (Based on か question particle.)) — 「肘が痛むんだよ。病院に行こうかな。」
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The elbow hurts and the speaker muses aloud about whether to go to the hospital — a self-directed wondering about the best next step.

facts.quizQuestion:
７月の上旬に、海に行くのは早い{___}。
(I [wonder] if the first week (or so) of July is too early to go to the beach.)
facts.explanation: かな (I wonder.. (Based on か question particle.)) — ７月の上旬に、海に行くのは早いかな。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The speaker is unsure whether early July is too early for the beach — かな voices that personal, uncertain wondering.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___}会いに行ったのに、友達はいませんでした。
(I [took the effort] to go and see my friend, but he wasn't there)
facts.explanation: せっかく (with effort) — せっかく会いに行ったのに、友達はいませんでした。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Expresses frustration at wasted effort — the speaker made the trip specifically to see their friend, but it was for nothing.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___}いい大学に入ったのだからよく勉強するつもりです。
(I [worked hard] to get into a good college, so I intend to study hard)
facts.explanation: せっかく (with effort) — せっかくいい大学に入ったのだからよく勉強するつもりです。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Marks that real work went into this achievement — getting into a good college was hard-won, so it shouldn't be wasted.

facts.quizQuestion:
人が{___}作ってくれた料理を食べないのは失礼だ。
(It's impolite not to eat food which someone has [taken the trouble] of preparing for you.)
facts.explanation: せっかく (with effort) — 人がせっかく作ってくれた料理を食べないのは失礼だ。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Emphasizes that effort deserves respect — someone put real work into cooking that meal, so refusing it is rude.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___}彼女に手紙を書いたのに、送るのを忘れちゃった。
(I [took the effort] to write a letter to her and forgot to send it.)
facts.explanation: せっかく (with effort) — せっかく彼女に手紙を書いたのに、送るのを忘れちゃった。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The effort makes the mistake more painful — the speaker took the time to write the letter, which makes forgetting to send it worse.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___}母親が買ってきたシャツをどうしても着ようとしなかった。
(After all of [the trouble] my mother went to buy the shirt, I did not wear it.)
facts.explanation: せっかく (with effort) — せっかく母親が買ってきたシャツをどうしても着ようとしなかった。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Highlights ingratitude after a caring gesture — the mother went out of her way to buy the shirt, and the child wouldn't wear it.

facts.quizQuestion:
今着いた{___}です
(I am [just now] arriving (Lit: now arrival time is)
facts.explanation: ところ (place, time, occasion) — 今着いたところです
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: I've just now arrived at this exact moment — ところ pinpoints the precise present instant of arrival rather than a general time.

facts.quizQuestion:
あなたはそれを終えましたか。それどこか、今始めた{___}です。
(Have you finished it? On the contrary, I've [just] begun.)
facts.explanation: ところ (place, time, occasion) — あなたはそれを終えましたか。それどこか、今始めたところです。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: ところに、ところへ、ところを
facts.grammarNote: I've only just started, right at this moment — ところ marks the exact point in time: the action just began, contrasting with 'finished.'

facts.quizQuestion:
あなたは昨晩私が彼女と二人っきりでいる{___}を見つけて驚いたにちがいない。
(You must have been surprised to find me with her alone [last night].)
facts.explanation: ところ (place, time, occasion) — あなたは昨晩私が彼女と二人っきりでいるところを見つけて驚いたにちがいない。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: ところに、ところへ、ところを
facts.grammarNote: You found him at the specific moment he was alone with her — ところ identifies the precise scene/occasion that was discovered.

facts.quizQuestion:
あなたは当分の間私の{___}にいても良い。
(You may stay with me [for the time being].)
facts.explanation: ところ (place, time, occasion) — あなたは当分の間私のところにいても良い。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Staying 'at my place' means the speaker's physical location — ところ functions as a concrete location reference here, not a time expression.

facts.quizQuestion:
家が狭いものです{___}大きい家具は置けません
(The house is small (narrow) [so / therefore] you cant put big furniture in (it).)
facts.explanation: ものだから (so, therefore (explaining something unavoidable)) — 家が狭いものですから大きい家具は置けません
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The structure uses ものです + から together — a formal way to state that the small space is the unavoidable reason large furniture won't fit.

facts.quizQuestion:
一人っ子な{___}わがままに育ててしまいました。
(It ((s)he) was an only child and [so] grew selfish)
facts.explanation: ものだから (so, therefore (explaining something unavoidable)) — 一人っ子なものだからわがままに育ててしまいました。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Presents being an only child as the natural, built-in reason for being spoiled — it's framed as an unavoidable outcome.

facts.quizQuestion:
遅くなってごめんね。道路が込んでいたもん{___}。
(I'm sorry I'm late, [because] I got caught in a traffic jam.)
facts.explanation: ものだから (so, therefore (explaining something unavoidable)) — 遅くなってごめんね。道路が込んでいたもんだから。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The spoken form もんだから provides an apologetic explanation — the traffic jam is offered as the unavoidable reason for being late.

facts.quizQuestion:
上着を脱いでもいいですか。暑いものです{___}。
(Can I take my jacket off? [Because] I'm feeling a bit hot.)
facts.explanation: ものだから (so, therefore (explaining something unavoidable)) — 上着を脱いでもいいですか。暑いものですから。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Uses ものです + から as a polite explanation — feeling hot is the natural, hard-to-ignore reason behind the request.

facts.quizQuestion:
僕は何でも嗅げれない。どうやら風邪を引っちゃったよ。{___}。
(It seems I have caught a cold and [so] cant smell anything.)
facts.explanation: ものだから (so, therefore (explaining something unavoidable)) — 僕は何でも嗅げれない。どうやら風邪を引っちゃったよ。ものだから。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Presents catching a cold as the natural, unavoidable cause — the inability to smell is an inevitable result of being sick.

facts.quizQuestion:
その{___}
([on the other hand])
facts.explanation: 反面 (on the other hand) — その反面
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: ところに、ところへ、ところを
facts.grammarNote: その反面 is a fixed phrase meaning 'on the other hand' — 反面 introduces a contrasting side to what was just said.

facts.quizQuestion:
普通兄は優しい{___}、卑しいところもある。
(My older brother is usually kind, but [on the other hand] he can be mean.)
facts.explanation: 反面 (on the other hand) — 普通兄は優しい反面、卑しいところもある。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The brother is kind, but on the other hand has a mean side — 反面 presents the second trait as the flip side of the first.

facts.quizQuestion:
その仕事は十分にお金になるが、その{___}１日に１２時間働かなくてはならない。
(The job pays well, but [on the other hand] I have to work twelve hours a day.)
facts.explanation: 反面 (on the other hand) — その仕事は十分にお金になるが、その反面１日に１２時間働かなくてはならない。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The job pays well, but on the other hand demands 12 hours — 反面 introduces the negative trade-off that comes with the positive.

facts.quizQuestion:
この布は水に強い{___},熱には弱い.
(This cloth is strong in water [on the other hand], is vulnerable to heat.)
facts.explanation: 反面 (on the other hand) — この布は水に強い反面,熱には弱い.
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Strong against water but weak to heat — 反面 sets up the contrasting weakness on the other side of the strength.

facts.quizQuestion:
インターネットは子供にとって役に立つ学習ツールだが、{___}、しっかりその使い方を指導しない子供がポルノや沫ﾍシーンの画像に簡単に接してしまうことになりかねない。
(The internet is a useful learning tool for children. [On the other hand], it can expose them to pornographic or violent images if they are allowed to use it unsupervised.)
facts.explanation: 反面 (on the other hand) — インターネットは子供にとって役に立つ学習ツールだが、反面、しっかりその使い方を指導しない子供がポルノや沫ﾍシーンの画像に簡単に接してしまうことになりかねない。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The internet is useful for learning, but on the other hand poses risks — 反面 introduces the contrasting danger that accompanies the benefit.

facts.quizQuestion:
彼が彼女にキスし{___}した時、,お母さんが 部屋に入って来た（のでキスできなかった）。
(He was [trying to] kiss his girlfriend when his mom walked in the room. (so I did not get to kiss her))
facts.explanation: ようとする (Try to do something, but the nuance is that it was unsuccessful.) — 彼が彼女にキスしようとした時、,お母さんが 部屋に入って来た（のでキスできなかった）。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: He was mid-action — leaning in for a kiss — when his mom walked in and stopped it. The attempt was real but never completed.

facts.quizQuestion:
「ちょっと目まい{___}わ。」「顔色が悪いぞ。一日休んだほうがいいよ。」
(" I [feel] sort of dizzy." "You look pale. You'd better take a day off.")
facts.explanation: がする (to affect the senses (to taste, to smell of, to feel like, to give the sensation of etc.)) — 「ちょっと目まいがするわ。」「顔色が悪いぞ。一日休んだほうがいいよ。」
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Physical sensations like dizziness attach naturally here — the body is experiencing something, and がする marks that involuntary perception.

facts.quizQuestion:
このスープは魚の味{___}。
(This soup [tastes] of fish.)
facts.explanation: がする (to affect the senses (to taste, to smell of, to feel like, to give the sensation of etc.)) — このスープは魚の味がする。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Taste is a sense that 'happens' to you — the fish flavor registers in the soup, and がする captures that sensory experience.

facts.quizQuestion:
この部屋はガスのにおい{___}。
(This room [smells] of gas.)
facts.explanation: がする (to affect the senses (to taste, to smell of, to feel like, to give the sensation of etc.)) — この部屋はガスのにおいがする。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Smell is something your senses detect passively — the gas odor is being picked up by the speaker's nose.

facts.quizQuestion:
あなたも血のにおい{___}な.
(I [smell] blood on you too.)
facts.explanation: がする (to affect the senses (to taste, to smell of, to feel like, to give the sensation of etc.)) — あなたも血のにおいがするな.
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: An ominous physical detection — the smell of blood is something perceived with the senses, not a thought or opinion.

facts.quizQuestion:
誰{___}。
(Who is it [?])
facts.explanation: だい (? (for casual questions)) — 誰だい。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: A very casual 'who is it?' — だい makes the question sound familiar and slightly gruff, typical of informal male speech.

facts.quizQuestion:
何をこそこそしているん{___}。
(What are you doing sneaking around [?])
facts.explanation: だい (? (for casual questions)) — 何をこそこそしているんだい。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: A parent or older person asking what someone is sneaking around doing — だい adds that informal, slightly scolding tone.

facts.quizQuestion:
いつまで起きているん{___}。早く寝なさい！
(Why are you still up [?] Just go to bed!)
facts.explanation: だい (? (for casual questions)) — いつまで起きているんだい。早く寝なさい！
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Someone is staying up late and being told to go to bed. だい makes the question feel like an annoyed, casual demand for an explanation.

facts.quizQuestion:
その優しげな目の奥にあるのは本当に何を知りたいん{___}?
(What do you wanna know in your cute eyes [?])
facts.explanation: だい (? (for casual questions)) — その優しげな目の奥にあるのは本当に何を知りたいんだい?
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: A curious, slightly searching question about what someone really wants to know. だい gives it that casual, probing informal feel.

facts.quizQuestion:
もう〜。また皿を壊して{___}ったか。
(Good grief. (That) another plate (you've) [gone and] broken?)
facts.explanation: しまう (仕舞う・終う・了う) (Vte, (Definitely, completely [often to ones regret] do V)) — もう〜。また皿を壊してしまったか。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.targetLanguageWord: しまう (仕舞う・終う・了う)
facts.reading: しまう (仕舞う・終う・了う)
facts.pronunciation: しまう (仕舞う・終う・了う)
facts.grammarNote: Breaking yet another plate is an unwanted, completed mishap — the regret/completion nuance of しまう fits the speaker's frustration perfectly.

facts.quizQuestion:
夢に耽ったり、生きることを忘れて{___}のはよくない。
(Becoming absorbed in a dream, [forgetting] to live is no good.)
facts.explanation: しまう (仕舞う・終う・了う) (Vte, (Definitely, completely [often to ones regret] do V)) — 夢に耽ったり、生きることを忘れてしまうのはよくない。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.targetLanguageWord: しまう (仕舞う・終う・了う)
facts.reading: しまう (仕舞う・終う・了う)
facts.pronunciation: しまう (仕舞う・終う・了う)
facts.grammarNote: Forgetting to live is presented as a negative outcome one drifts into — しまう marks this as an undesirable completed (or recurring) state.

facts.quizQuestion:
あなたは笑われて{___}でしょう。
(You will be [laughed at] completely.)
facts.explanation: しまう (仕舞う・終う・了う) (Vte, (Definitely, completely [often to ones regret] do V)) — あなたは笑われてしまうでしょう。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.targetLanguageWord: しまう (仕舞う・終う・了う)
facts.reading: しまう (仕舞う・終う・了う)
facts.pronunciation: しまう (仕舞う・終う・了う)
facts.grammarNote: Being laughed at is an outcome the speaker sees as unfortunate and definite — しまう signals this undesirable result will fully come to pass.

facts.quizQuestion:
電車の中に財布を落として{___}った。
(I [lost] my wallet in the train.)
facts.explanation: しまう (仕舞う・終う・了う) (Vte, (Definitely, completely [often to ones regret] do V)) — 電車の中に財布を落としてしまった。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.targetLanguageWord: しまう (仕舞う・終う・了う)
facts.reading: しまう (仕舞う・終う・了う)
facts.pronunciation: しまう (仕舞う・終う・了う)
facts.grammarNote: Losing the wallet was an accidental, regrettable completed action — しまう captures both the finality and the 'oh no' feeling of the loss.

facts.quizQuestion:
医者{___}田中さん
(The Tanaka san who [is] a doctor.)
facts.explanation: である (To be, is (formal equivalent of だ [da])) — 医者である田中さん
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: This is a formal way to say 'who is a doctor' before a name, like a title. である gives the sentence a written or elevated tone.

facts.quizQuestion:
そう{___}としても、この場合は別でしょう。
(Even if that [is] correct, would it not have to be different in this case?)
facts.explanation: である (To be, is (formal equivalent of だ [da])) — そうであるとしても、この場合は別でしょう。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The sentence sets up a hypothetical — 'even if that is so' — and である provides the formal version of 'to be' inside the conditional.

facts.quizQuestion:
いつも正直{___}ということは、容易なことではない。
(To [be] always honest is not easy.)
facts.explanation: である (To be, is (formal equivalent of だ [da])) — いつも正直であるということは、容易なことではない。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The sentence is a formal statement about the challenge of always being honest. である carries the written, philosophical tone this statement needs.

facts.quizQuestion:
ボンドの映画を見{___}な〜。
(I [wanna see] the Bond movie!)
facts.explanation: たい (Vm, wanting to do ___) — ボンドの映画を見たいな〜。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The speaker really wants to see that movie — たい attached to the verb stem expresses this personal desire directly.

facts.quizQuestion:
この車を買い{___}んですが、お金が無い。
(I [want to buy] this car but I don't have the money.)
facts.explanation: たい (Vm, wanting to do ___) — この車を買いたいんですが、お金が無い。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The desire to buy the car is genuine, but money is the obstacle. たい shows the want clearly even when it can't be fulfilled.

facts.quizQuestion:
あなたは何をし{___}のですか。
(What would you [like to do]?)
facts.explanation: たい (Vm, wanting to do ___) — あなたは何をしたいのですか。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: ということですね (と云う事ですね)
facts.grammarNote: This is asking what the other person would like to do — たい on the verb stem asks about their personal wish or preference.

facts.quizQuestion:
公園{___}歩きました。
(I walked [through] the park.)
facts.explanation: を (area passed through (通過点)) — 公園を歩きました。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Marks the park as the space being moved through — you walked within and across the park, not just to it.

facts.quizQuestion:
12歳のとき、私は川{___}泳いで渡ることができた。
(I could swim [across] the river when I was twelve.)
facts.explanation: を (area passed through (通過点)) — 12歳のとき、私は川を泳いで渡ることができた。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The river is the space being crossed — swimming across it means moving through that space from one side to the other.

facts.quizQuestion:
この列車はニューヨークとボストン間{___}走っている。
(This train runs [between] New York and Boston.)
facts.explanation: を (area passed through (通過点)) — この列車はニューヨークとボストン間を走っている。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The route between cities is what the train travels through — を marks that ongoing path of movement.

facts.quizQuestion:
お忙しいところ{___}お出で頂きありがとうございます。
(Thank you very much for coming [despite] the fact that you were busy.)
facts.explanation: を (area passed through (通過点)) — お忙しいところをお出で頂きありがとうございます。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Marks the busy situation as what was 'moved through' to get here — your busyness was the obstacle you overcame to come.

facts.quizQuestion:
部屋{___}出た。
(I [left] the room.)
facts.explanation: を ((marks point of departure) 出発点) — 部屋を出た。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Marks the room as the place being left — を with a departure verb shows you exited from that location.

facts.quizQuestion:
大阪{___}出発する。
(He [leaves] Osaka.)
facts.explanation: を ((marks point of departure) 出発点) — 大阪を出発する。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Osaka is the city being departed from — を marks it as the starting point that is being left behind.

facts.quizQuestion:
去年日本{___}離れた。
(I [left] Japan last year.)
facts.explanation: を ((marks point of departure) 出発点) — 去年日本を離れた。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Japan is the place being left — the verb 離れる means to separate from, and を marks what you're separating from.

facts.quizQuestion:
１９００年に彼はイギリス{___}去った。
(In 1900 he [left] England.)
facts.explanation: を ((marks point of departure) 出発点) — １９００年に彼はイギリスを去った。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: England is the country he departed — を with a verb of leaving marks the place that was left behind.

facts.quizQuestion:
窓が開け{___}。
(The window has been [left open] (purposely).)
facts.explanation: て形+ある (て在る・て有る) (Vte something has been purposely put into a state) — 窓が開けてある。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.targetLanguageWord: て形+ある (て在る・て有る)
facts.grammarNote: Someone intentionally left the window in an open state — てある describes an object left in a condition as a result of a purposeful action.

facts.quizQuestion:
窓が閉め{___}。
(The window has been [closed] (purposely).)
facts.explanation: て形+ある (て在る・て有る) (Vte something has been purposely put into a state) — 窓が閉めてある。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.targetLanguageWord: て形+ある (て在る・て有る)
facts.grammarNote: The window was deliberately closed and remains that way — てある shows the resulting closed state was purposely created and is still in effect.

facts.quizQuestion:
窓を（は）開け{___}。
(I [left] the window open (purposely).)
facts.explanation: て形+ある (て在る・て有る) (Vte something has been purposely put into a state) — 窓を（は）開けてある。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.targetLanguageWord: て形+ある (て在る・て有る)
facts.grammarNote: The speaker intentionally left the window open — with を, てある focuses on the subject's deliberate action of putting something into that state.

facts.quizQuestion:
窓を（は）閉め{___}。
(I [kept] the window closed (purposely).)
facts.explanation: て形+ある (て在る・て有る) (Vte something has been purposely put into a state) — 窓を（は）閉めてある。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.targetLanguageWord: て形+ある (て在る・て有る)
facts.grammarNote: The speaker purposely kept the window shut — てある signals the closed state was intentionally maintained, not something that just happened.

facts.quizQuestion:
新聞を買っ{___}。
(I'll go buy the newspaper [and come back].)
facts.explanation: て形+くる (て来る) (Do something, and return) — 新聞を買ってくる。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Going to buy the newspaper implies you'll return afterward — てくる attaches to an action done away from home with the expectation of coming back.

facts.quizQuestion:
医者に行って薬をもらっ{___}ました。
(I went to the doctor and got some medicine [and came back with it].)
facts.explanation: て形+くる (て来る) (Do something, and return) — 医者に行って薬をもらってきました。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The speaker went to the doctor, got medicine, and came back — てきた captures both the completed away-action and the return to the speaker's base.

facts.quizQuestion:
日本語が少しずつ分かっ{___}
(Little by little, I [came to] understand Japanese.)
facts.explanation: て形+くる (て来る) (Do something, and return) — 日本語が少しずつ分かってきた
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Understanding grew gradually over time and has arrived at the present — てきた expresses a slow change that progressed up to now, not a sudden event.

facts.quizQuestion:
物価が下がっ{___}。
(Prices continue to fall [as time goes on].)
facts.explanation: て形+くる (て来る) (Do something, and return) — 物価が下がってくる。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Prices continuing to fall describes an ongoing trend moving toward the present — てくる marks a gradual change unfolding in the current direction.

facts.quizQuestion:
スイスとい{___}何をイメージしますか？
([When you hear] 'Switzerland' what does it invoke in your mind?)
facts.explanation: と言うと (when you talk of, speaking of, when you say) — スイスというと何をイメージしますか？
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Mentioning 'Switzerland' prompts a mental image. と言うと sets up that 'when you hear this topic' trigger for asking what comes to mind.

facts.quizQuestion:
なぜクビにならなかったか{___}彼には超能力があり、彼の予知で数々の難事件が解決をみたからだ
(To say [the reason] why he wasn't fired, having psychic powers, he solved many difficult cases with his foresight.)
facts.explanation: と言うと (when you talk of, speaking of, when you say) — なぜクビにならなかったかと言うと彼には超能力があり、彼の予知で数々の難事件が解決をみたからだ
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The speaker explains why he wasn't fired by introducing the reason. と言うと frames this as 'to state the reason' — a clarifying pivot.

facts.quizQuestion:
何かとい{___}あいつはいつも口を挟みたがる。
(Whenever something comes up, that guy always wants to interject.)
facts.explanation: と言うと (when you talk of, speaking of, when you say) — 何かというとあいつはいつも口を出そうとする。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Whatever comes up, that person inserts himself. と言うと works here as 'whenever anything is brought up,' covering any topic.

facts.quizQuestion:
これはあくまでも{___}以外のなにものでもない。
(This is only [a plan] and nothing more than that.)
facts.explanation: 予定 (be planning to do~) — これはあくまでも予定以外のなにものでもない。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The thing being talked about is nothing more than a plan — not a done deal. 予定 is precisely the word for that scheduled, planned-but-not-guaranteed state.

facts.quizQuestion:
明日この宿題を終わる{___}です。
(I [plan to] finish this assignment tomorrow.)
facts.explanation: 予定 (be planning to do~) — 明日この宿題を終わる予定です。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: と伝えていただけませんか
facts.grammarNote: Finishing the assignment tomorrow is scheduled and intended. 予定 marks this as a concrete plan, not just a vague wish.

facts.quizQuestion:
来週帰国する{___}です。
(I [plan to] go back to my home country next week.)
facts.explanation: 予定 (be planning to do~) — 来週帰国する予定です。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Going home next week is the plan. 予定 signals this is scheduled and intended, not yet decided in the moment.

facts.quizQuestion:
来年彼女と結婚する{___}です。
(next year I [am planning to] marry her.)
facts.explanation: 予定 (be planning to do~) — 来年彼女と結婚する予定です。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Marrying her next year is a definite plan the speaker has made. 予定 marks it as something scheduled and committed to.

facts.quizQuestion:
着{___}もいい？
(Is it ok if i [try it on] to see?)
facts.explanation: て形+みる (to try (out)) — 着てみてもいい？
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Trying on clothes to see how they fit is a classic 'do it and see' situation — てみる expresses doing something as an experiment or trial.

facts.quizQuestion:
わたし　の　かいしゃ　は　ほん　を　{___}。
(My company [published] a book.)
facts.explanation: 出す、出して、出した (to pay, to place, to issue, to stick out, to put forth, to serve) — わたし　の　かいしゃ　は　ほん　を　出した。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The company published a book in the past — 出した is the past tense form used here for the act of issuing or putting out something.

facts.quizQuestion:
ごみ　を　{___}。
([Take out] the garbage.)
facts.explanation: 出す、出して、出した (to pay, to place, to issue, to stick out, to put forth, to serve) — ごみ　を　出す。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Taking out the garbage is the act of removing it from a space — 出す here means to put out or remove, not a verb-stem compound.

facts.quizQuestion:
お金　を　{___}　から　買って　ください。
(I will [pay] (for it), please buy it.)
facts.explanation: 出す、出して、出した (to pay, to place, to issue, to stick out, to put forth, to serve) — お金　を　出す　から　買って　ください。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Paying the money first is the condition before buying — 出す means to put out or produce money, used for paying or providing funds.

facts.quizQuestion:
何かき{___}ことがある？
(Is there something you're [worried about]?)
facts.explanation: 気になる (thinking about, concerned about, worried about) — 何かきになることがある？
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: と伝えていただけませんか
facts.grammarNote: The blank completes になる — something is on the speaker's mind or causing them concern right now.

facts.quizQuestion:
A:なんでその変なスタイルにしたの？ Ｂ：何か、{___}よ
(A: Why did you go with that weird style? B: Well, the feeling [just came to me]!)
facts.explanation: 気になる (thinking about, concerned about, worried about) — A:なんでその変なスタイルにしたの？ Ｂ：何か、気になるよ
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Describes a spontaneous pull of attention — something about that style caught their interest or got under their skin.

facts.quizQuestion:
子供の教育のことが{___}。
(I'm [worried about] my child's education.)
facts.explanation: 気になる (thinking about, concerned about, worried about) — 子供の教育のことが気になる。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: A worry that keeps nagging — the child's education stays on the parent's mind and causes ongoing concern.

facts.quizQuestion:
バスを降りよ{___}、後ろから押された。
(I was [about to] get off the bus, when someone pushed me from behind.)
facts.explanation: おうとすると (A voluntary action stopped just before being completed) — バスを降りようとすると、後ろから押された。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: ところに、ところへ、ところを
facts.grammarNote: The push happened at the exact moment of stepping off — the action was cut off right before completion by an outside event.

facts.quizQuestion:
出かけよ{___}、父に呼ばれた。
(I was just [about to] go out, when dad called me.)
facts.explanation: おうとすると (A voluntary action stopped just before being completed) — 出かけようとすると、父に呼ばれた。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: ところに、ところへ、ところを
facts.grammarNote: Dad's call arrived at the precise instant of heading out the door, interrupting the action just before it could happen.

facts.quizQuestion:
彼女に告白しよ{___}、突然友達が来ました。
(when i [try to] propose her suddenly friends come.)
facts.explanation: おうとすると (A voluntary action stopped just before being completed) — 彼女に告白しようとすると、突然友達が来ました。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: ところに、ところへ、ところを
facts.grammarNote: The confession was already in motion when a friend's sudden arrival broke the moment and stopped it from happening.

facts.quizQuestion:
僕は君{___}速く歩けません。
(I can't walk [as fast as] you can.)
facts.explanation: ほど-2 (to the extent of; to the extent that ~; (not as) ~ as ~ ; about [REL. bakari; kurai]) — 僕は君ほど速く歩けません。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Sets you as the upper limit the speaker can't reach — I fall short of your walking speed, using you as the benchmark.

facts.quizQuestion:
ジェニファー{___}よく勉強する学生はいない。
(There is no student who studies [as hard as] Jennifer.)
facts.explanation: ほど-2 (to the extent of; to the extent that ~; (not as) ~ as ~ ; about [REL. bakari; kurai]) — ジェニファーほどよく勉強する学生はいない。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Places Jennifer at the extreme end of the scale — no other student comes close to matching her level of studying.

facts.quizQuestion:
私は西田さんがあれ{___}ピアノが弾けるは思わなかった。
(I didn't think that Mr. Nishida could play the piano [that well]. (lit. to that extent))
facts.explanation: ほど-2 (to the extent of; to the extent that ~; (not as) ~ as ~ ; about [REL. bakari; kurai]) — 私は西田さんがあれほどピアノが弾けるは思わなかった。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Uses あれほど to point at the level actually demonstrated — the speaker didn't expect Mr. Nishida's ability to reach that extent.

facts.quizQuestion:
ルイスさんの日本語は太田先生も外国人だと気づかなかった{___}上手です。
(Mr. Lewis's Japanese is [so good that] even Prof. Ota didn't notice he was a foreigner.)
facts.explanation: ほど-2 (to the extent of; to the extent that ~; (not as) ~ as ~ ; about [REL. bakari; kurai]) — ルイスさんの日本語は太田先生も外国人だと気づかなかったほど上手です。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Measures fluency against an impressive real-world outcome — his Japanese is good to the degree that even an expert was fooled.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___}事故は起こるものだ。
([No matter what], accidents will happen.)
facts.explanation: どうしても (absolutely, surely, by all means, no matter what, at any cost; after all) — どうしても事故は起こるものだ。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: States a universal truth — no matter what precautions people take, accidents are still going to happen.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___}痩せたい
(I'd like to lose weight [no matter what].)
facts.explanation: どうしても (absolutely, surely, by all means, no matter what, at any cost; after all) — どうしても痩せたい
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: あまり　+　動詞(否定形)
facts.grammarNote: Expresses a strong personal determination — losing weight is a goal the speaker is absolutely committed to, no matter what it takes.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___}上手になりたいです。
(I want to improve [no matter what].)
facts.explanation: どうしても (absolutely, surely, by all means, no matter what, at any cost; after all) — どうしても上手になりたいです。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: あまり　+　動詞(否定形)
facts.grammarNote: States an unwavering desire to improve — the speaker is fully committed to getting better, no conditions or excuses.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___}試験に合格したいです
(I wanna pass examination [no matter what].)
facts.explanation: どうしても (absolutely, surely, by all means, no matter what, at any cost; after all) — どうしても試験に合格したいです
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: あまり　+　動詞(否定形)
facts.grammarNote: Expresses intense motivation — passing the exam is a non-negotiable goal for the speaker.

facts.quizQuestion:
私はきっと変{___}人だと思われてる。
(I'm sure I'm thought of as an [odd] person.)
facts.explanation: な ((particle used with na-adjectives)) — 私はきっと変な人だと思われてる。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: 変 is a na-adjective, so it needs な to connect it to the noun 人. This is the standard way na-adjectives modify nouns.

facts.quizQuestion:
彼女はきれい{___}足をしている。
(She has [beautiful] legs.)
facts.explanation: な ((particle used with na-adjectives)) — 彼女はきれいな足をしている。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: きれい is a na-adjective describing the legs. The な connects it directly to the noun that follows.

facts.quizQuestion:
静か{___}レストランに行きましょう。
(Let's go to a [quiet] restaurant.)
facts.explanation: な ((particle used with na-adjectives)) — 静かなレストランに行きましょう。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: 静か is a na-adjective. It needs な to link it to the noun レストラン — this is how na-adjectives modify nouns.

facts.quizQuestion:
日本へ行った{___}のころは、日本語がわからなくて困りました。
(When I was [a newcomer] to Japan, I had trouble understanding Japanese.)
facts.explanation: ばかり (have just done something; have just finished doing something’) — 日本へ行ったばかりのころは、日本語がわからなくて困りました。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Just having arrived in Japan, the speaker was a newcomer — ばかり marks the period just after coming, when everything was unfamiliar.

facts.quizQuestion:
食べた{___}だから、ちょっと待ってください。
(I [just] ate so please wait a bit.)
facts.explanation: ばかり (have just done something; have just finished doing something’) — 食べたばかりだから、ちょっと待ってください。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The speaker just ate a moment ago — ばかり expresses the very recent completion of eating, which is why waiting is needed.

facts.quizQuestion:
働き始めた{___}のとき、僕も間違えたよ。
(When I had [just] begun working, I made some mistakes too.)
facts.explanation: ばかり (have just done something; have just finished doing something’) — 働き始めたばかりのとき、僕も間違えたよ。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Just having started work, mistakes were natural — ばかり marks the period immediately after beginning, when everything is new.

facts.quizQuestion:
2つのことを一度にしよう{___}はいけません。
(Don't [try to] do two things at a time.)
facts.explanation: として (try to) — 2つのことを一度にしようとしてはいけません。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: しまう (仕舞う・終う・了う)
facts.grammarNote: Captures the moment of attempting something — the speaker is actively trying to do two things at once, which is the bad idea being warned against.

facts.quizQuestion:
このような（子供の）態度は、自分たちがある程度独立していることを示そう{___}の結果なのである。
(This is because they are [trying to] prove they are somewhat independent.)
facts.explanation: として (try to) — このような（子供の）態度は、自分たちがある程度独立していることを示そうとしての結果なのである。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: ところに、ところへ、ところを
facts.grammarNote: Frames the behavior as driven by a goal — the children's attitude comes from their effort to appear independent.

facts.quizQuestion:
アイスクリームを食べよう{___}、スプーンがないことに気が付いた。これでは食べようがない。どうしよう。
(When they were [trying to] eat ice cream, they noticed they didn't have a spoon. There's no way to eat. What should we do?)
facts.explanation: として (try to) — アイスクリームを食べようとして、スプーンがないことに気が付いた。これでは食べようがない。どうしよう。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Describes the exact moment before an action — just as they were about to eat the ice cream, the missing spoon became a problem.

facts.quizQuestion:
{___}笑い。
(An [artificial] smile.)
facts.explanation: わざとらしい (fake, pretending) — わざとらしい笑い。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The smile looks forced and fake. わざとらしい sits before the noun 笑い to describe it as something performed, not genuine.

facts.quizQuestion:
彼の{___}話し方が嫌いだ。
(I don't like his [affected] manner of speaking.)
facts.explanation: わざとらしい (fake, pretending) — 彼のわざとらしい話し方が嫌いだ。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: His speaking style comes across as put-on and unnatural — わざとらしい captures that affected, over-the-top quality.

facts.quizQuestion:
彼のけがするのは、{___}ですね
(It's like he is [faking] his injury.)
facts.explanation: わざとらしい (fake, pretending) — 彼のけがするのは、わざとらしいですね
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: The injury seems staged rather than real. わざとらしい expresses that suspicious, too-convenient quality of his behavior.

facts.quizQuestion:
明日{___}それを済ませてしまうべきです。
(You ought to have done it [by] tomorrow.)
facts.explanation: までに (by __[day, date, time, etc.]__) — 明日までにそれを済ませてしまうべきです。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: ところに、ところへ、ところを
facts.grammarNote: There's a deadline of tomorrow for finishing it — までに sets a time limit by which the action must be completed.

facts.quizQuestion:
お昼{___}戻らないといけない。
((I) need to be back [by] noon.)
facts.explanation: までに (by __[day, date, time, etc.]__) — お昼までに戻らないといけない。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: ところに、ところへ、ところを
facts.grammarNote: Noon is the deadline for returning — までに marks a point in time that serves as the cutoff, not just a general 'when.'

facts.quizQuestion:
書類を夜{___}ださなければなりません。
(Document must be submitted [by] evening.)
facts.explanation: までに (by __[day, date, time, etc.]__) — 書類を夜までにださなければなりません。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: ところに、ところへ、ところを
facts.grammarNote: The document submission has a cutoff of evening — までに sets evening as the boundary by which the action must be done.

facts.quizQuestion:
野菜を食べ{___}。
(You [must] eat your vegetables.)
facts.explanation: なければなりません (must ____; really should _____; it won't do if you don't ____.) — 野菜を食べなければなりません。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
facts.grammarNote: Eating vegetables is framed as a firm requirement, not just advice. なければなりません is the polite form for a strong obligation.

facts.quizQuestion:
お店で卵を買わ{___}。
(I [have to] buy eggs at the store.)
facts.explanation: なければなりません (must ____; really should _____; it won't do if you don't ____.) — お店で卵を買わなければなりません。なければなりません。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: と伝えていただけませんか
facts.grammarNote: Buying eggs is something that has to happen — it's a necessary errand. なければなりません states that obligation in polite speech.

facts.quizQuestion:
毎日日本語を勉強し{___}。
(I [must] study Japanese everyday.)
facts.explanation: なければなりません (must ____; really should _____; it won't do if you don't ____.) — 毎日日本語を勉強しなければなりません。
facts.visualDescription: Japanese grammar study card with sentence completion
distractors: と伝えていただけませんか
